title: Cow and Fields
description: Bessie is out grazing on the farm, which consists of $$$n$$$ fields connected by $$$m$$$ bidirectional roads. She is currently at field $$$1$$$, and will return to her home at field $$$n$$$ at the end of the day. The Cowfederation of Barns has ordered Farmer John to install one extra bidirectional road. The farm has $$$k$$$ special fields and he has decided to install the road between two different special fields. He may add the road between two special fields that already had a road directly connecting them. After the road is added, Bessie will return home on the shortest path from field $$$1$$$ to field $$$n$$$. Since Bessie needs more exercise, Farmer John must maximize the length of this shortest path. Help him!
input_spec: The first line contains integers $$$n$$$, $$$m$$$, and $$$k$$$ ($$$2 \le n \le 2 \cdot 10^5$$$, $$$n-1 \le m \le 2 \cdot 10^5$$$, $$$2 \le k \le n$$$)  — the number of fields on the farm, the number of roads, and the number of special fields. The second line contains $$$k$$$ integers $$$a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_k$$$ ($$$1 \le a_i \le n$$$)  — the special fields. All $$$a_i$$$ are distinct. The $$$i$$$-th of the following $$$m$$$ lines contains integers $$$x_i$$$ and $$$y_i$$$ ($$$1 \le x_i, y_i \le n$$$, $$$x_i \ne y_i$$$), representing a bidirectional road between fields $$$x_i$$$ and $$$y_i$$$. It is guaranteed that one can reach any field from every other field. It is also guaranteed that for any pair of fields there is at most one road connecting them.
output_spec: Output one integer, the maximum possible length of the shortest path from field $$$1$$$ to $$$n$$$ after Farmer John installs one road optimally.

routes.name: Two BFS + greedy pairing
routes.description: Run BFS from both endpoints, then greedily pick the pair of special fields whose added edge maximizes the shortest path.

observations.title: New path through the added edge has a fixed form
observations.description: If we add an edge between special fields a and b, any shortest path using that edge goes 1 -> a -> b -> n or 1 -> b -> a -> n. Its length is min(dist(1,a) + 1 + dist(b,n), dist(1,b) + 1 + dist(a,n)). The overall shortest path is the minimum of this and the original shortest path.

hints.nudge: Think about what a shortest path that uses the new edge looks like. It must enter through one endpoint and leave through the other.
hints.question: If a shortest path uses the new edge (a, b), what are the only two ways it can traverse it? What is the total length in each case?
hints.formal: A path using edge (a,b) has length dist(1,a) + 1 + dist(b,n) or dist(1,b) + 1 + dist(a,n). The new shortest path is min(original, min of these two).

observations.title: BFS from both endpoints gives all needed distances
observations.description: Since all edge weights are 1, BFS from node 1 gives dist(1, v) for all v, and BFS from node n gives dist(v, n) for all v. These two arrays are all we need to evaluate any candidate edge.

hints.nudge: The graph is unweighted. What is the most efficient way to compute shortest paths from a single source in an unweighted graph?
hints.question: How many BFS runs do you need to know the distance from node 1 and to node n for every special field?
hints.formal: Run BFS from node 1 to get d1[v], and BFS from node n to get dn[v]. For any pair (a,b), the new path length is min(d1[a] + 1 + dn[b], d1[b] + 1 + dn[a]).

observations.title: Maximize the minimum of two expressions
observations.description: For a pair (a, b) the path through the new edge costs min(d1[a] + dn[b], d1[b] + dn[a]) + 1. We want to maximize this over all pairs. The min selects the cheaper direction, so we want both directions to be as expensive as possible.

hints.nudge: You want to maximize min(X, Y) where X and Y depend on the pair. When is min(X, Y) as large as possible?
hints.question: For a fixed pair (a,b), what determines which of the two directions is chosen? Can you express the condition X >= Y in terms of the distances?
hints.formal: d1[a] + dn[b] >= d1[b] + dn[a] simplifies to d1[a] - dn[a] >= d1[b] - dn[b]. So if we define val[v] = d1[v] - dn[v], the smaller-val node determines the direction.

observations.title: Sort by d1[v] - dn[v] to decouple the optimization
observations.description: Define val[v] = d1[v] - dn[v] for each special field v. Sort by val. For a pair where val[a] <= val[b], the path cost is d1[a] + dn[b] + 1. This decouples: a contributes d1[a] and b contributes dn[b].

hints.nudge: Can you assign a single number to each special field that determines the optimal pairing order?
hints.question: If you sort the special fields by d1[v] - dn[v], and always pair a left element with a right element, which distances contribute to the cost?
hints.formal: Sort special fields by val[v] = d1[v] - dn[v]. For a < b in sorted order, cost = d1[a] + dn[b] + 1. To maximize, sweep right on b while tracking max d1[a] over all a to the left.

observations.title: Linear scan with running maximum yields optimal pair
observations.description: After sorting by val, iterate through special fields. Maintain a running maximum of d1[a] for all previously seen fields. For each new field b, compute d1[a_best] + dn[b] + 1. The answer is the minimum of the original shortest path and the best such value.

hints.nudge: After sorting, you need the best pair. Do you need to check all O(k^2) pairs, or can you do better?
hints.question: As you scan left to right, what single value from the left side matters? Can you maintain it incrementally?
hints.formal: Keep max_d1 = max of d1[a] over all a seen so far. For each b, update best = max(best, max_d1 + dn[b] + 1). Then update max_d1 = max(max_d1, d1[b]). Answer = min(d1[n], best).
